bull.b: Extremely low debt and sizable equity provide durable financial flexibility, lowering refinancing and interest risk. This supports multi-year clinical programs, M&A or licensing optionality, and gives management time to de-risk assets before needing dilutive financing.

Nearly half-a-billion in cash increases runway for pivotal trials and operations, reducing immediate financing pressure. This funding lets Oruka advance Phase 2b/other studies and negotiate partnerships from a position of strength over the next 2–6+ months.

High complete-clearance rates and durable PK/PD suggesting once-yearly dosing could provide a durable competitive advantage in psoriasis. If replicated in later trials, this supports premium positioning, pricing power, and easier uptake versus existing IL-23p19 biologics.

bear.b: No product revenue and materially increasing losses mean long-term value hinges entirely on clinical success and future commercialization. This raises execution risk and potential dilution if programs delay or need additional financing before revenue generation.

Sustained, rising cash outflows across periods create ongoing funding requirements even with current cash. Continued high burn increases reliance on equity markets or partnerships within 2–3 years and can compress optionality if trial timelines extend.

Contractual dosing limits for IBD constrain Oruka’s ability to pursue that indication or combine ORKA-001 in trials/partnerships for years. This reduces near-term strategic flexibility, could limit partnering options, and delays possible market expansion into IBD.
